names.japanese_name: 架橋カルボキシメチルセルロースナトリウム

natural_occurrence.description: Crosslinked sodium carboxymethyl cellulose is a semi-synthetic derivative of cellulose. It's created by chemically modifying plant cellulose with carboxymethyl groups, then crosslinking the polymer chains. It's processed wood pulp or cotton that has been extensively chemically modified.

manufacturing.description: Produced by first creating sodium carboxymethylcellulose (E466), then chemically crosslinking the polymer chains using crosslinking agents. The crosslinks create a three-dimensional network structure that doesn't dissolve in water but swells dramatically. This swelling property makes it excellent for tablet disintegration in pharmaceuticals and for absorbing water in food applications.

other_uses.pharmaceutical: Tablet disintegrant (most common use - helps tablets break apart in the stomach, brand name Ac-Di-Sol).

eu.conditions: Approved for use in specified food categories; less commonly used in foods than regular CMC (E466).

usa.notes: More commonly used in pharmaceuticals than foods

japan.japanese_regulation_name: 架橋カルボキシメチルセルロースナトリウム

commonly_found_in: Pharmaceutical tablets (helps them dissolve quickly in stomach)
Less commonly used directly in foods compared to regular CMC (E466)
fun_facts: Unlike regular CMC which dissolves in water, crosslinked CMC swells up like a sponge - it can absorb many times its weight in water!
Like all celluloses, it's chemically modified wood pulp or plant fiber - processed sawdust that's been crosslinked into a network
It's the secret ingredient that makes tablets dissolve quickly in your stomach - the crosslinked structure swells rapidly, breaking the tablet apart
Completely indigestible by humans, so it passes through unchanged as dietary fiber
The crosslinking prevents it from dissolving, but the swelling action is what makes it useful - it's like a molecular spring that expands when wet
